explicit_text: I was born in Denver, Colorado, and grew up there before moving around the state for work. Denver was where I went to school, attending Columbia, Skinner, and North, and later West High School. My early experiences in Denver shaped my understanding of business and community, especially as I saw practices like lagniappe in local grocery stores.

explicit_text: I came to Estes Park in 1931 when I took over a store here, after working in Boulder, Fort Collins, and Loveland. Estes Park became my home and business base for many years, including running my own store and serving on the city council for 32 years, including two terms as mayor during the 1950s and beyond. The community and its development, including events like the Adams Tunnel and Trail Ridge Road openings, have been central to my life.

explicit_text: I was running a chain grocery store in Boulder when I was just eighteen years old, which was my first steady start in the grocery business around 1927. Boulder was an important stepping stone for me before I was called to work for the Honor Brite Company and moved on to other towns in Colorado. It was a place where I gained early experience that prepared me for later opportunities.

explicit_text: My brother Chester and I formed Brodie Brothers when we took over the store next to the Estes Park Bank in 1936. Chester was mostly a silent partner since he was interested in trucking, while I ran the grocery business. We owned and operated the store together for over twenty-one years before building a new building in 1957 where I still am today.

explicit_text: I served in the infantry during World War II and was stationed at Camp Roberts for almost two years. They kept me there without shipping me overseas, and I was even talked into staying by an old man who spent two hours with me on the field. My service at Camp Roberts was a significant period in my life during the war years.

explicit_text: One of the most exciting events during my time in Estes Park was the drilling of the Adams Tunnel in the late 1930s. Despite the economic challenges of the time, we managed to transition smoothly through that period.

explicit_text: I have vivid memories of the opening ceremonies for Trail Ridge Road, where I often shook hands with the mayor on the other side. Sometimes we had to drive around to get there, other times we entered through Rainbow Curve with the gate closed behind us, bulldozers opening the road. These ceremonies were a tradition for many years and a highlight of community life.

explicit_text: During my time in public service, I was involved with zoning efforts that started piece by piece rather than all at once, which I thought was the right approach. I recall attending a meeting where Larimer County tried to zone the entire county at once, and I advised against it. The cooperation between the city and county was important for orderly growth and development.

explicit_text: I believe the National Park Service is the main attraction that keeps many of us here in Estes Park. Their expansion and presence have been vital to the town's economy and identity. I also had a good relationship with David Canfield, the superintendent, and his wife Helen, who contributed a lot to the community and the Park.

explicit_text: When Alf Landon ran for President of the United States, he had his campaign headquarters here in Estes Park at McGraw Ranch. I remember that period well because it was the last time I rode a horse down Main Street with him. It was quite an event and a memorable moment in local political history.
